content.problemStatement:
For a binary string SSS, define f(S)=f(S) = f(S)= longest non-decreasing subsequence of SSS. For example, f(0011)=4f(0011) = 4f(0011)=4 and f(0101)=3f(0101) = 3f(0101)=3.

You are given a binary string SSS of length NNN, and an integer KKK.

Consider splitting SSS into KKK substrings, T1,T2,…,TKT_1, T_2, \ldots, T_KT1​,T2​,…,TK​ such that S=T1+T2+…+TKS = T_1 + T_2 + \ldots + T_KS=T1​+T2​+…+TK​. The score of such a split is defined as min⁡(f(T1),f(T2),…,f(TK))\min(f(T_1), f(T_2), \ldots, f(T_K))min(f(T1​),f(T2​),…,f(TK​)).

Find the maximum possible score possible by choosing a suitable split.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains 222 integers NNN and KKK - the length of the string, and the number of parts to split into.
The second line contains SSS - a binary string of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum possible score.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : We can split as 10+11010 + 11010+110, f(10)=1f(10) = 1f(10)=1 and f(110)=2f(110) = 2f(110)=2.
Test Case 2 : We can keep the string intact as K=1K = 1K=1 and we do not have to split it. f(10110)=3f(10110) = 3f(10110)=3.
